title: Add a Multiple of Ten to a Two-Digit Number
description: Add 10, 20, 30, etc. to a two-digit number by increasing the tens digit while keeping the ones digit the same.
condition: An addition expression where one addend is a multiple of 10 and the other is a two-digit number (e.g., 74 + 20, 47 + 10).
response: Increase the tens digit by the number of tens in the multiple of 10; keep the ones digit unchanged.
label: Principle

title: Add Ones Without Making a New Ten
description: Add ones digits when their sum is less than 10, keeping the tens unchanged.
condition: An addition expression where the ones digits sum to less than 10 (e.g., 23 + 6).
response: Add the ones digits and keep the tens digit the same.
label: Principle

title: Add Three Numbers Within 20
description: Add three numbers within 20, possibly using grouping strategies.
condition: A word problem or expression with three addends within 20 (e.g., 7 + 6 + 3).
response: Combine two addends (often to make 10) and add the third to find the total.
label: Principle

title: Compare Two-Digit Numbers by Place Value
description: Compare two-digit numbers by examining tens first, then ones if needed.
condition: Two two-digit numbers presented with <, >, or = to determine.
response: Compare tens digits; if equal, compare ones digits; choose the correct symbol.
label: Principle

title: Compose and Decompose Teen Numbers
description: Understand a teen number as 10 and some ones.
condition: A teen number (11–19) is given or referenced.
response: Express the number as 10 and some ones (e.g., 16 = 10 + 6).
label: Principle

title: Count Back to Subtract
description: Subtract by counting backward from the starting number.
response: Count backward from the first number to the second and determine the number of counts.
label: Principle

title: Count On to Find Difference
description: Find a difference by counting up from the smaller number to the larger number.
condition: A comparison or subtraction situation within 20 (e.g., 15 − 11).
response: Count up from the smaller number to the larger and record the number of counts.
label: Principle
example: From $11$ to $15$: 12, 13, 14, 15 → 4 counts, so the difference is $4$.

title: Evaluate Equation as True or False
description: Determine whether a given addition or subtraction equation is correct.
condition: An equation with given numbers and an equals sign (e.g., 6 + 6 = 14).
response: Compute both sides and decide if they are equal.
label: Principle
example: $6 + 6 = 12$, and $12 \ne 14$, so the equation is false.

title: Interpret Compare (Difference Unknown) Word Problem
description: Translate a comparison story problem into a subtraction or counting-on equation to find how many more.
condition: A word problem asking how many more one quantity is than another.
response: Represent the situation with subtraction or counting-on and compute the difference.
label: Principle
example: Jada has 17 and Noah has 9. Compute $17 - 9 = 8$.

title: Represent Tens and Ones with Objects
description: Interpret groups of ten objects and single objects as tens and ones to determine total value.
condition: A visual model showing tens (e.g., rods) and ones (e.g., cubes).
response: Count tens and ones and combine to state the total number.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Non-verbal classification assumes reliance on visual pattern recognition.

title: Use Transitive Reasoning to Compare Lengths
description: Use two given comparisons to determine a third comparison.
condition: Given A > B and B > C for lengths.
response: Conclude A > C and equivalent shorter/longer statements.
label: Principle
example: If hallway > flagpole and flagpole > snake, then hallway > snake.

title: Verify Accurate Measurement Without Gaps
description: Recognize that length units must be placed end-to-end without gaps or overlaps for accurate measurement.
condition: A length measured with units that have visible gaps or overlaps.
response: State that the measurement is inaccurate and explain units must be contiguous.
label: Principle
example: If 7 cubes have gaps between them, they do not correctly measure the object’s length.
